facts.label: FIRST AFRICAN VICTORY
facts.story: On 2 June 1978, Tunisia trailed Mexico 1–0 at halftime of their World Cup debut. Coach Abdelmajid Chetali did something legendary: he hurled the national flag onto the dressing-room floor and walked out without a word. Whatever the message, it worked — Ali Kaabi equalized in the 55th minute, then Néjib Ghommidh and Mokhtar Dhouieb added two more, and Tunisia won 3–1, becoming the first African or Arab nation ever to win a World Cup match. President Habib Bourguiba told the squad they had done the work of fifty ambassadors.

facts.label: EAGLES OF CARTHAGE
facts.story: Tunisia's crest didn't always feature an eagle. The bird arrived after 14 February 2004, when the Carthage Eagles beat Morocco 2–1 at the 7 November Stadium in Tunis to win their first Africa Cup of Nations, Francileudo Santos and Ziad Jaziri scoring the goals. The triumph rewired the team's identity — pressed into a new badge, stitched onto sleeves, chanted from the stands ever since as 'Eagles of Carthage.' Before that, Tunisians simply called their side by its colors, the red and white unchanged since independence.

1978.headline: First African World Cup Win

annotations.label: KAABI SPARKS COMEBACK
annotations.story: Trailing Mexico by a first-half penalty, Tunisia stormed back in the second half. Ali Kaabi leveled in the 55th minute — the first World Cup goal ever scored by a Tunisian — before Néjib Ghommidh and Mokhtar Dhouieb added late goals to seal a stunning 3–1 win that opened doors for African football everywhere.

annotations.label: HOLDING THE CHAMPIONS
annotations.story: Three days after that historic win, Tunisia held defending champions West Germany scoreless for ninety minutes, salvaging a 0–0 draw. It was enough to finish third in Group 2 above Mexico — and pundits noticed: Africa's contingent at the World Cup increased for the very next tournament.

annotations.label: ADIDAS FLAG COLORS
annotations.story: Adidas supplied Tunisia's kit for their 1978 debut: a white home shirt and red away change strip lifted directly from the national flag, with no crest embellishment beyond the federation badge — a plain template Tunisia wore into the history books against Mexico.

1978.homeColors: Red shirt with cream V-neck collar and cuffs, white adidas three stripes on the sleeves (Adidas)
1978.awayColors: White shirt with red round-neck collar, red adidas three stripes on the sleeves, red cuffs (Adidas)

annotations.label: SOUAYAH'S CONSOLATION
annotations.story: Down 0–2 to England and 0–1 to Colombia, Tunisia's World Cup return after twenty years threatened total blankness until the final group match. Skander Souayah converted a 12th-minute penalty against Romania, salvaging a 1–1 draw and Tunisia's only goal of France '98 — a lone flare in an otherwise dark comeback tournament.

annotations.label: LOTTO'S RETURN JERSEY
annotations.story: Twenty years after their Adidas debut, Tunisia returned to the World Cup in a Lotto-made red home shirt, worn just once, in the opening loss to England at Marseille's Stade Vélodrome — a shirt now considered one of the rarer World Cup jerseys among collectors.

annotations.label: KASPERCZAK'S RETURN
annotations.story: Polish coach Henryk Kasperczak, who had built his reputation managing clubs across French football, ended Tunisia's twenty-year World Cup absence by qualifying them for France '98 — the country's second-ever appearance, two decades after their pioneering 1978 debut.

1998.homeColors: Red shirt with white polo collar and jagged white shoulder-to-side graphic pattern (Lotto)
1998.awayColors: White shirt with thin red pinstripes, red geometric shoulder patterns, red-trimmed V-neck collar (Lotto)

annotations.label: BOUZAIENE LEVELS BELGIUM
annotations.story: Beaten by Russia in the opener, Tunisia responded against Belgium when Raouf Bouzaiene struck in the 17th minute for a 1–1 draw — the team's only goal of a tournament played entirely on Japanese soil. It wasn't enough; a loss to co-host Japan sent Tunisia out at the bottom of Group H.

annotations.label: PUMA'S RED PANELS
annotations.story: Puma dressed Tunisia in white for 2002, with a red V-neck collar and red panels running from the underarms down the sides — a cleaner, more angular update from the plain shirts of the 1990s, worn entirely on Japanese soil since all of Group H's matches were hosted there.

annotations.label: A EUROPEAN-BUILT SQUAD
annotations.story: Captain Adel Sellimi, who had played in France's Ligue 1 and Germany's Bundesliga with Werder Bremen, led a 2002 squad built increasingly around Tunisian pros based in Europe — a shift from the largely domestic-league roster that had made history in 1978.

2002.homeColors: White shirt with red V-neck collar and red panels under the arms extending to the sides (Puma)
2002.awayColors: Red shirt with white side and underarm panels, gold Puma branding (Puma)

2006.headline: Jaïdi's Last-Gasp Equalizer

annotations.label: JAÏDI'S STOPPAGE HEADER
annotations.story: Ziad Jaziri put Tunisia ahead inside 23 minutes against Saudi Arabia, but two second-half goals threatened to sink the opener. Centre-back Radhi Jaïdi rose in second-half stoppage time — the 90+2nd minute — to head home a scrambling equalizer, salvaging a 2–2 draw in Tunisia's tournament opener.

annotations.label: MNARI STUNS SPAIN
annotations.story: Jawhar Mnari drilled Tunisia ahead inside eight minutes against a Spain side stacked with Raúl, Casillas and Puyol. Spain leveled inside the hour, Fernando Torres restored the lead, and a 90th-minute penalty finished it at 3–1 — leaving Tunisia needing a win over Ukraine they never got.

annotations.label: LEMERRE'S DOUBLE CROWN
annotations.story: Roger Lemerre, sacked by France after their disastrous 2002 title defense, rebuilt his career in Tunis — winning the 2004 Africa Cup of Nations to become the first coach ever to win two different continental championships, then leading Tunisia to the 2006 World Cup.

2006.homeColors: White shirt with red trim on collar and cuffs, faded eagle graphic across the chest, gray accents (Puma)
2006.awayColors: Red shirt with subtle tonal graphic pattern, white Puma logos and crest (Puma)

2018.headline: Ending a 40-Year Wait

annotations.label: KHAZRI ENDS THE DROUGHT
annotations.story: Fakhreddine Ben Youssef and Wahbi Khazri struck either side of the hour mark to beat Panama 2–1 in Tunisia's final group match — their first World Cup victory in forty years, since that 3–1 win over Mexico in 1978. It meant nothing for qualification; Tunisia had already been eliminated. It meant everything for history.

annotations.label: KANE BREAKS TUNISIAN HEARTS
annotations.story: Ferjani Sassi's penalty had Tunisia level with England inside twenty-one minutes, and the equalizer held deep into stoppage time. Then Harry Maguire flicked on a corner and Harry Kane, left unmarked at the back post, headed home the winner in the first minute of injury time — the cruelest possible ending.

annotations.label: UHLSPORT'S DOTTED FADE
annotations.story: German manufacturer Uhlsport's 2018 shirt ran a dotted gradient pattern fading in from the sides of an otherwise plain white home jersey, red crew collar and cuffs completing the look — a design that drew mixed reviews during Uhlsport's three-year run supplying the federation.

2018.homeColors: White shirt with red trim on the crew-neck collar and cuffs, fading dotted pattern on the flanks (Uhlsport)
2018.awayColors: Red shirt with white trim on collar and cuffs, textured dot pattern down the sides (Uhlsport)

2022.headline: Toppling the World Champions

annotations.label: KHAZRI TOPPLES FRANCE
annotations.story: Wahbi Khazri drilled a low shot into the bottom corner in the 58th minute to beat defending champions France 1–0 — Tunisia's first-ever World Cup win over a European team. Two minutes later, Australia scored against Denmark in the other match, eliminating Tunisia anyway. Khazri walked off a national hero and retired from international football weeks later.

annotations.label: TWO NEAR MISSES
annotations.story: Christian Eriksen forced a save from goalkeeper Aymen Dahmen, and moments later Andreas Cornelius rose unmarked at the far post but could only crash his header off the frame of the goal. Both misses kept the opener goalless. Aïssa Laïdouni's display in midfield earned him the match's man-of-the-match award.

annotations.label: THE CARTHAGINIAN CUIRASS
annotations.story: Kappa's 2022 kits carried a tonal print of the Ksour Essef cuirass, a 3rd-century-BC bronze breastplate unearthed in Tunisia in 1909 and linked to Hannibal's Second Punic War campaigns — home and away shirts swapped the red-and-white balance, while the third kit rendered it in olive green.

2022.homeColors: White shirt with red Kappa graphic print, crest, and trim (Kappa)
2022.awayColors: Red shirt with white Kappa graphic print and crest (Kappa)
